questions.question: A "bull flag" slopes:
options: Downward (against the prior uptrend) during consolidation

questions.question: A "bear flag" slopes:
options: Upward (against the prior downtrend) during consolidation

questions.question: A "rectangle" pattern is a:
options: Consolidation between horizontal support and resistance

questions.question: A rectangle can break in:
The longer the rectangle lasts, the:
A "cup and handle" pattern consists of:
options: A rounded bottom (cup) followed by a small pullback (handle)
A sharp V bottom followed by a flag
A head and shoulders with a handle
A rectangle followed by a triangle

questions.question: The cup and handle pattern is:
The measured move of a cup and handle is:
options: Depth of the cup added to the breakout at the handle's high
Depth of the cup subtracted from the handle

questions.question: An "inverse cup and handle" would be:
The volume pattern in a valid head and shoulders top should show:
options: Lower volume on the right shoulder compared to the left shoulder
Higher volume on the right shoulder

questions.question: In a head and shoulders bottom, volume should be:
options: Higher on the breakout above the neckline

questions.question: In a double top, volume is typically:
options: Lower on the second top than the first

questions.question: In a double bottom, volume is typically:
options: Higher on the breakout above the middle peak

questions.question: A "pullback" after a breakout often retests:
options: The opposite side of the pattern

questions.question: A "throwback" in a bullish breakout is when price:
options: Returns to test the broken resistance as support

questions.question: A "failure" of a pattern occurs when:
options: The anticipated breakout does not happen or reverses quickly

questions.question: A "failed breakout" often leads to:
options: A sharp move in the opposite direction
A continuation in the breakout direction

questions.question: The "30/70 rule" for patterns suggests:
Chart patterns are generally more reliable on:
The "neckline" in a head and shoulders pattern can be:
A "complex head and shoulders" has:
options: Multiple shoulders on one or both sides

questions.question: A "rounded bottom" typically takes:
options: More time to form than V bottoms
The same time as all patterns

questions.question: The "handle" in a cup and handle should ideally:
options: Not retrace more than 50% of the cup's depth
Have higher volume than the cup

questions.question: "Bullish flags" are usually preceded by:
options: A sharp price increase on high volume

questions.question: "Bearish flags" are usually preceded by:
options: A sharp price decrease on high volume

questions.question: A "pennant" pattern is different from a flag because of its:
The "measured move" of any continuation pattern is based on:
Chart patterns are most reliable when:
options: Confirmed with volume and follow-through price action

questions.question: A "pattern failure" that reverses can become:
options: An even stronger signal in the opposite direction

questions.question: A "bust" pattern occurs when:
options: A head and shoulders pattern fails and continues the original trend
A triangle breaks in the expected direction
A flag reaches its measured target

questions.question: The "bust" pattern is also known as a:
"Bull trap" refers to:
options: A breakout above resistance that fails and reverses down
A breakdown below support that reverses up

questions.question: "Bear trap" refers to:
options: A breakdown below support that fails and reverses up
A breakout above resistance that reverses down

questions.question: Pattern recognition is most effective when combined with:
"Price action" refers to:
options: The movement of price over time without indicators

questions.question: Volume represents:
options: The number of shares/contracts traded in a period
The price change in a period

questions.question: Volume is considered the "fuel" behind price movement because:
options: High volume confirms the strength of a move
Volume always leads price by exactly 3 days

questions.question: A price increase on increasing volume is:
options: Bullish and suggests strong buying interest

questions.question: A price increase on decreasing volume is:
A price decrease on increasing volume is:
options: Bearish and suggests strong selling interest

questions.question: A price decrease on decreasing volume is:
options: Potentially weak and may reverse up

questions.question: Volume climax (extremely high volume) often indicates:
options: Exhaustion and potential reversal near tops or bottoms

questions.question: "On-Balance Volume" (OBV) is an indicator that:
options: Adds volume on up days and subtracts on down days to show cumulative buying/selling pressure

questions.question: When OBV diverges from price (price up, OBV flat/down), it suggests:
options: Weakness in the uptrend, potential reversal

questions.question: Volume precedes price means:
options: Volume changes often happen before price changes
Price changes always happen before volume

questions.question: A "low volume" pullback in an uptrend is considered:
options: Healthy and often leads to trend continuation

questions.question: A "high volume" pullback in an uptrend is considered:
options: Potentially concerning and could lead to reversal

questions.question: "Unusual volume" relative to average suggests:
options: Institutional interest and potential significant move

questions.question: A "volume dry-up" (very low volume) often precedes:
The "wick" (shadow) of a candlestick represents:
options: The distance between the real body and the high/low

questions.question: A "big spread" (large range) candlestick indicates:
A "small spread" (narrow range) candlestick indicates:
A "narrow range day" (NRD) often precedes:
A "wide range day" (WRD) after a narrow range suggests:
options: Potential start of a new move or trend

questions.question: "Inside day" occurs when:
options: Today's high is lower than yesterday's high AND today's low is higher than yesterday's low
Today's range is larger than yesterday's

questions.question: An "inside day" often signals:
"Outside day" occurs when:
options: Today's range completely engulfs yesterday's range

questions.question: An "outside day" can be:
options: Bullish or bearish depending on close relative to open

questions.question: A "key reversal day" is an outside day where:
options: The close is opposite to the direction of the prior trend (e.g., close down after an uptrend)
The close is in the same direction as the trend

questions.question: Key reversal days are significant at:
"Opening price" as support or resistance is significant because:
options: It represents a key reference level for the session

questions.question: "First hour high and low" often act as:
options: Initial support and resistance for the day
Only relevant for the first 5 minutes

questions.question: A "break" of first hour high or low is often used as:
options: An early trend signal for the session

questions.question: "Yesterdays high and low" are significant because:
options: They represent areas of prior rejection/acceptance

questions.question: "Yesterdays close" is a key reference level because:
options: It represents settlement price and overnight sentiment

questions.question: "Overnight gap" above yesterday's high suggests:
options: Strong bullish sentiment but watch for fill

questions.question: "Overnight gap" below yesterday's low suggests:
options: Strong bearish sentiment but watch for fill

questions.question: A "gap fill" occurs when price returns to:
options: Close the gap area (previous day's close range)

questions.question: "Trapped traders" occur when:
options: Price moves against their position after a breakout/fakeout

questions.question: "Trapped traders" often fuel further moves when:
options: They are forced to cover their losing positions

questions.question: A "buyer's exhaustion" is shown by:
options: Long upper wicks, small real bodies at highs

questions.question: A "seller's exhaustion" is shown by:
options: Long lower wicks, small real bodies at lows

questions.question: A "low volume" breakout is often:
options: Not reliable and may be false

questions.question: A "high volume" breakout is often:
"Volume divergence" occurs when:
options: Price makes a new high/lows but volume declines
Volume increases but price is flat

questions.question: Volume divergence suggests:
"Tick volume" is used in markets where:
options: Actual volume is not available (e.g., forex)

questions.question: Tick volume measures:
options: The number of price changes (ticks) in a period
The actual number of shares traded

questions.question: A "trendline" break with a volume spike suggests:
Price action "key levels" are often found at:
options: Whole numbers, prior highs/lows, pivot points, and moving averages

questions.question: The "lunch hour" (midday) often sees:
"End of day" volume spikes are often due to:
"Price action trading" relies primarily on:
options: Naked charts with support/resistance and candlestick patterns

questions.question: A common risk management rule is to risk:
"Position sizing" depends on:
options: Account size, risk per trade, and stop-loss distance

questions.question: A "stop-loss" order is used to:
options: Limit potential loss on a trade

questions.question: A "take-profit" order is used to:
options: Lock in profits at a target level

questions.question: "Risk-reward ratio" of 1:3 means:
A positive expectancy system requires:
options: Win rate × reward > loss rate × risk

questions.question: "Backtesting" a strategy means:
"Forward testing" (paper trading) means:
options: Testing in real-time without real money

questions.question: "Confirmation" in technical analysis means:
options: Waiting for additional signals to validate a setup
Entering immediately on the first signal

questions.question: "Confluence" in trading refers to:
options: Multiple factors pointing to the same conclusion

questions.question: "Overbought" on RSI (above 70) does NOT guarantee:
"Oversold" on RSI (below 30) does NOT guarantee:
In strong trends, RSI can remain:
options: Overbought or oversold for extended periods

questions.question: "Divergence" is strongest when it is:
options: Hidden or regular with momentum confirmation

questions.question: "Hidden divergence" suggests:
"Regular divergence" suggests:
The "psychological" level of 100 (or 10000) is often:
options: Support or resistance due to human round-number bias

questions.question: The ultimate purpose of technical analysis is to:
options: Identify high-probability trading opportunities with defined risk

questions.question: A chart shows a sequence of swing lows where each swing low is higher than the previous one, and each swing high is also higher than the previous one. What type of trend does this swing structure describe?

A chart shows a sequence where each swing high is lower than the previous swing high, and each swing low is also lower than the previous swing low. What type of trend does this swing structure describe?
A price level has been tested three times without a closing price above it. On the fourth test, price closes above that level. What does this closing price above the level describe?
A price level has been tested three times without a closing price below it. On the fourth test, price closes below that level. What does this closing price below the level describe?
A chart shows a sequence of candles moving in the same direction. Each candle body is progressively smaller than the one before it. What does this shrinking body size describe?
A chart shows three consecutive swing highs. Each swing high is lower than the one before it. What does this sequence of swing highs describe?
Price closes above a resistance level that had held for several sessions. On the following session, price closes back below that same resistance level. What does this two-session sequence describe?
Price closes below a support level that had held for several sessions. On the following session, price closes back above that same support level. What does this two-session sequence describe?
A chart shows a sequence of candles where each candle body is larger than the one before it. What does this pattern of increasing body size describe?
A chart shows a candle whose high is lower than the previous candle's high, and whose low is higher than the previous candle's low. What describes this candle relative to the previous one?

A chart shows a sequence of higher highs and higher lows. Price then forms a swing high that is lower than the previous swing high, followed by a swing low that is lower than the previous swing low. What does this combined sequence describe?
options: A structural shift from higher highs and higher lows to lower highs and lower lows
A continuation of the prior higher high and higher low sequence
A consolidation phase within the prior move
A single lower low within an otherwise intact upward sequence

questions.question: A chart shows a sequence of lower highs and lower lows. Price then forms a swing high that is higher than the most recent swing high. What does this single swing high describe?
options: A lower high — continuation of the prior sequence
A higher high — a break in the sequence of lower highs
An equal high — no change to the prior sequence

questions.question: A chart shows price making higher highs and higher lows. After forming a new swing high, price retraces downward before resuming upward movement. What does this retracement describe?

A chart shows price moving upward with swing highs that are progressively higher and swing lows that are also progressively higher. A line connecting the highs runs parallel to a line connecting the lows. What does this structure describe?
A candle has a small body positioned near its low, with a wick above the body that is at least twice the length of the body. What type of candle is this?

Price repeatedly fails to close above a horizontal level over several sessions. Price then closes above that level. On the following session, price returns to that level and holds above it without closing below. What has occurred at this level?
options: The level has been confirmed as persistent resistance
The level has been eliminated as a significant price point
Role reversal has occurred — the prior resistance is now acting as support
The level has become a neutral zone between buyers and sellers

questions.question: Price makes a sharp upward move, then forms a narrow downward-sloping channel of smaller candles. Price then breaks above the upper boundary of that channel. What does this breakout signal?
options: A resumption of the prior upward move
A reversal of the prior upward move
A period of consolidation with no directional bias
A failure of the continuation pattern

questions.question: The RSI (Relative Strength Index) for a price chart crosses above the level of 30 after registering multiple sessions below it. What does this RSI behavior indicate?
options: Price has moved into overbought territory
Price may be recovering from an oversold condition
Price is likely to continue declining
The RSI reading has no directional significance at this level

questions.question: After a series of declining candles, price reaches a prior support level. A candle then forms with a small body near its high and a long lower wick at least twice the length of the body. What does this candle signal at this support level?
options: A sign that downward momentum is accelerating at this level
An indecision signal with no directional bias
A bearish continuation signal at the support level
A potential bullish reversal signal at this support level

questions.question: After several sessions of rising prices, a small bullish candle forms. On the following session, a larger bearish candle opens above the prior candle's close and closes below the prior candle's open, with its body fully covering the prior candle's body. What does this two-candle sequence signal?
options: A continuation of the prior upward move
A potential reversal of the prior upward move
An indecision signal with no directional implication
A consolidation phase before the upward move resumes

questions.question: After several sessions of declining prices, a small bearish candle forms. On the following session, a larger bullish candle opens below the prior candle's close and closes above the prior candle's open, with its body fully covering the prior candle's body. What does this two-candle sequence signal?
options: A potential reversal of the prior downward move
A continuation of the prior downward move
An indecision signal with no directional implication
A consolidation phase before the downward move resumes

questions.question: A price level has acted as support across multiple sessions, with price bouncing from it on three separate occasions. Price then closes below that level for several sessions. On a subsequent session, price returns to that level from below and fails to close above it. What does this combined sequence most likely demonstrate?
options: The level remains valid support — price will likely recover above it on the next session
The level has been permanently eliminated as a significant price reference
Role reversal has occurred — the prior support is now acting as resistance
The level has become a neutral zone where buyers and sellers are temporarily balanced

questions.question: Price makes a sharp downward move across several sessions. It then forms a narrow upward-sloping channel of smaller candles that retrace a portion of the decline. Price then breaks below the lower boundary of that channel. What does this breakdown most likely signal?
options: A reversal of the prior downward move — the pattern indicates buyers are regaining control
A period of consolidation with no directional resolution
A failure of the continuation pattern — further analysis is required before acting
A resumption of the prior downward move

questions.question: Price makes a new swing high that is higher than the previous swing high. At the same time, the RSI reading at the new swing high is lower than the RSI reading at the previous swing high. What does this relationship between price and RSI most likely indicate?
options: The RSI reading confirms the strength of the upward move — higher price with any RSI level is bullish
Bearish divergence — upward momentum is weakening even as price reaches new highs
The RSI is in overbought territory, which always signals an immediate reversal
The lower RSI reading has no significance when price is making new highs

questions.question: Price has been making higher highs and higher lows for an extended period. It then forms a swing high that is lower than the prior swing high. On the following move, price closes below the most recent prior swing low. What does this two-event sequence most likely signal?
options: A potential structural shift from uptrend to downtrend
A temporary pullback within the uptrend — the prior structure remains intact
A double bottom pattern indicating the uptrend will accelerate
A consolidation phase — the sequence is insufficient to determine trend direction

questions.question: Price breaks above a resistance level that has held for several weeks, closing clearly above it for three consecutive sessions. On the fourth session, price pulls back to that former resistance level. The session closes above that level without closing below it. What does this pullback behavior confirm?
options: The breakout has failed — price returning to the prior level signals a reversal
The level has been permanently eliminated and has no further relevance
The breakout is confirmed — the former resistance is holding as new support on the retest
Price is forming a double top and is likely to reverse sharply below the breakout level

questions.question: After an upward move, price makes a swing high. It then pulls back to a lower level before advancing again to approximately the same high as the first swing high. Price then declines below the level of the pullback between the two highs. What does this three-part structure describe?
options: A continuation pattern signaling the upward move will resume
A flag pattern — a counter-trend pause before the move continues higher
A consolidation zone with no directional bias
A double top — a reversal pattern indicating upward momentum has stalled at this level

questions.question: A price chart shows several consecutive sessions where candle bodies are consistently small, upper and lower wicks are approximately equal in length, and the overall price range is narrowing compared to prior sessions. No clear direction is present in the sequence. What does this candle pattern describe?
options: Expanding momentum — small bodies with equal wicks indicate an imminent breakout in both directions
Price consolidation — a period of reduced volatility with no established directional bias
A reversal pattern — the decreasing range confirms the prior trend has ended
A flag pattern — the narrow candles indicate a pause before the prior trend resumes

questions.question: A trader enters a long position at $50.00. The stop-loss is placed at $48.50 and the take-profit target is placed at $54.50. What is the risk-to-reward ratio of this trade?
options: 1:1 — the stop distance and target distance are equal at $1.50 each
1:2 — the $4.50 target represents twice the $1.50 stop distance
1:3 — the $1.50 stop distance is one-third of the $4.50 target distance
3:1 — the trade risks three units for every one unit of potential profit

questions.question: A trader maintains a fixed risk of $500 per trade. In Trade A, the entry is $200 and the stop-loss is $195 — a $5 risk per share. In Trade B, the entry is $200 and the stop-loss is $198 — a $2 risk per share. To maintain exactly $500 risk in both trades, how does the required position size for Trade B compare to Trade A?
options: Trade B requires a larger position — a tighter stop demands proportionally more shares to maintain the same fixed dollar risk
Trade B requires a smaller position — a tighter stop indicates lower conviction and should be paired with reduced size
Both trades use the same position size — fixed dollar risk means identical position size regardless of stop distance
Trade B's position size cannot be determined without knowing the instrument's current volatility

questions.question: After holding below a resistance level at 1.3500 for eight months, EUR/USD breaks above it with three consecutive daily closes above 1.3500 and rallies to 1.3650. Price then pulls back toward 1.3500. On the retest, two consecutive daily sessions close at 1.3490 and 1.3485 — both below 1.3500 — before price continues lower. What does the retest behavior most likely indicate?
options: The retest confirms the breakout — minor closes below a breakout level are expected noise and do not invalidate the move
The level has become permanently neutral — price oscillating around 1.3500 means no directional conclusion can be drawn
A double bottom is forming at 1.3490 — the two closes below the level represent accumulation before a new upward move
The breakout has failed — consecutive closes below the former resistance on the retest indicate the level has not converted to support, invalidating the bullish thesis

questions.question: During an extended uptrend, price forms three peaks: a left peak at $82, a center peak at $94, and a right peak at $83. A support level connecting the two troughs between the peaks holds near $75. Price then breaks below $75 with a close below that level. What does this three-peak sequence and its breakdown most likely describe?
options: A triple top — three peaks at nearly identical price levels indicate exhausted buyers before a confirmed reversal
A head and shoulders pattern — a higher central peak flanked by two lower peaks, with a breakdown below the neckline, signals a potential reversal of the prior uptrend
A rising wedge — progressively different peak heights indicate converging trendlines characteristic of this continuation pattern
A broadening formation — the alternating peak heights and the neckline break confirm a megaphone pattern rather than a reversal

questions.question: Over ten sessions, a trading instrument's daily range compresses — each session's high-to-low range is smaller than the prior session's, candle bodies are consistently small and centered within each range, and the total price movement across all ten sessions is less than a typical single session from prior weeks. On the eleventh session, a candle forms with a range three times the ten-session average and closes in the upper 10% of the session's range. What does this sequence most likely indicate?
options: The compression phase has extended — the expansion candle on session 11 is a statistical outlier with no directional significance
The range compression is a distribution event — the expansion candle confirms institutional selling is executing above the range
A volatility expansion is emerging from a compression phase; the direction and close position of the expansion candle provide early evidence of upward directional bias
A compression lasting ten sessions guarantees a downward resolution — ranges of this duration always resolve bearishly before continuation

questions.question: A market is in a confirmed uptrend defined by higher highs and higher lows over four months. The most recent swing high is at 1.4800; the most recent prior swing low is at 1.4200. Price pulls back from 1.4800 and reaches 1.4250 before closing sessions above that level and resuming higher. What does this pullback most likely confirm about the trend?
options: The trend's higher-low structure remains intact — the pullback held above the prior swing low at 1.4200, preserving the sequence that defines the uptrend
The trend has reversed — any pullback reaching within 50 points of the prior swing low signals structural breakdown and an imminent downtrend
The pullback is a distribution event — the depth of the decline from 1.4800 to 1.4250 indicates institutional selling into the advance
The trend is confirmed exhausted — the failure to make a deeper low indicates buyers lack conviction for a sustained advance

questions.question: After eight consecutive declining sessions that have brought price to a well-established multi-year support zone, a doji candle forms. The doji's open and close are separated by 3 ticks, with an upper wick of 25 ticks and a lower wick of 28 ticks. The two preceding sessions were the two largest-range bearish candles of the entire decline. What does this doji formation most likely signal at this juncture?
options: A confirmed bullish reversal — a doji at multi-year support after large bearish candles is a definitive signal that the decline is complete
A confirmed bearish continuation — the doji after two large bearish candles indicates exhausted buyers who were unable to halt the decline
The doji is analytically irrelevant at support — single-candle signals only carry significance at resistance levels

A potential inflection point — the doji indicates buyer-seller equilibrium at a structurally significant level, with the preceding large bearish candles suggesting possible exhaustion; confirmation from subsequent sessions is required before committing directionally

questions.question: An instrument in a defined uptrend prints four consecutive bullish sessions near a prior swing high. Session 1: body covers 80% of the high-to-low range. Session 2: body covers 55%. Session 3: body covers 25%, with the upper wick covering 40% of the range. Session 4: body covers 15%, with the upper wick covering 55% of the range. Price has not yet closed above the prior swing high. What does this progression most likely indicate?
options: Accelerating bullish momentum — four consecutive bullish candles confirm sustained buying pressure regardless of individual body-to-range ratios
Weakening upward momentum near resistance — buyers are progressively less capable of sustaining gains to the close, and sellers are rejecting price with increasing force within each session
A reliable continuation pattern — declining body-to-range ratios over four sessions reliably precede a breakout in the trend direction
An indecision signal with no directional implication until price closes above or below the prior swing high

questions.question: During an uptrend, price reaches a region where a significant prior daily swing high exists. Over three sessions, the following sequence forms: Session 1 — a large bullish candle extending strongly into the resistance region. Session 2 — a small candle whose body is less than 30% of Session 1's body, with wicks in both directions. Session 3 — a large bearish candle that opens below the Session 2 close and closes at least 50% into the body of the Session 1 bullish candle. What does this three-session sequence at the resistance region most likely describe?
options: A bullish continuation — a small indecision candle between two large candles confirms the uptrend's strength before the advance resumes
A gap-fill scenario — the Session 2 indecision defines the consolidation target before the uptrend resumes from that level
An evening star pattern — a three-candle topping structure at resistance signaling a potential reversal of the uptrend
A double top forming — the small Session 2 candle represents the second peak, with the Session 3 decline confirming the top

questions.question: A stock completes a four-week uptrend on progressively increasing volume. It then enters a corrective phase: price retraces 38% of the prior rally over six weeks, volume during the correction consistently exceeds the average volume recorded during the uptrend, and the RSI never recovers above 50 during any intra-correction bounce. Which assessment is most consistent with trend continuation analysis?
options: The volume expansion and sub-50 RSI throughout the correction indicate bearish distribution — continuation of the prior uptrend is at risk
A 38% retracement is within acceptable structural limits; the extended duration and heavier volume confirm the trend is pausing healthily before continuation
The time-ratio violation — correction lasting longer than the impulse — is the sole signal required to classify this as a trend reversal
Volume and momentum oscillators during corrective phases are secondary factors; the 38% retracement alone determines whether trend continuation is likely
